topics.title: Basic Characteristics of Nonlinear Systems, Examples, State-Space Representation of Nonlinear Systems, Classification of Nonlinearities, Phase Plane Analysis: Concept of Phase Plane and Singular Points, Definition of Stability - Asymptotic Stability and Instability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET752 - NONLINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Characteristics of Nonlinear Systems, Examples, State-Space Representation of Nonlinear Systems, Classification of Nonlinearities, Phase Plane Analysis: Concept of Phase Plane and Singular Points, Definition of Stability - Asymptotic Stability and Instability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Nonlinear Systems

A **nonlinear system** is a dynamical system whose behavior is governed by nonlinear equations, meaning that the principle of superposition (additivity and homogeneity) does **not** apply. Mathematically, a system described by the operator $N[\cdot]$ acting on an input $u(t)$ to produce output $y(t)$ is nonlinear if and only if:

$$N[\alpha u_1(t) + \beta u_2(t)] \neq \alpha N[u_1(t)] + \beta N[u_2(t)]$$

for arbitrary scalar constants $\alpha, \beta$ and arbitrary inputs $u_1(t), u_2(t)$.

In contrast, a **linear time-invariant (LTI) system** strictly satisfies:

$$N[\alpha u_1(t) + \beta u_2(t)] = \alpha N[u_1(t)] + \beta N[u_2(t)]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEET752), a nonlinear control system is one in which at least one of the system parameters, components, or governing equations contains a nonlinear functional relationship (such as multiplication of variables, trigonometric functions, saturation, dead-zone, relay action, or higher-order terms beyond first degree).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine driving a car on a perfectly flat, straight road (linear behavior) versus driving on a hilly, winding mountain road (nonlinear behavior):

- On the **flat road**, a small, proportional turn of the steering wheel results in a small, predictable change in direction. Double the steering input, and the car's response doubles. This is **linear superposition**.
- On the **mountain road**, doubling the steering wheel input does **not** double the car's turning radius. The car's response also depends on speed, road curvature, gravity, and tire grip. The system exhibits **nonlinear characteristics** such as saturation (steering reaches a mechanical limit) and multi-valued response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples of Nonlinear Phenomena:**
> - **Saturation** in operational amplifiers or magnetic circuits
> - **Dead-zone** in servo motors due to static friction
> - **Backlash (hysteresis)** in gear trains
> - **Relay (on-off) action** in thermostat-controlled systems
> - **Square-law and cubic nonlinearities** in fluid dynamics and semiconductor devices

### 1.3 Basic Characteristics of Nonlinear Systems

The fundamental behavioral traits that distinguish nonlinear systems from their linear counterparts are summarized below:

- **Non-applicability of Superposition Principle:** The response to a sum of inputs is not equal to the sum of individual responses. This single property invalidates almost all classical linear tools (transfer functions, Bode plots, root locus) for direct application.

- **Dependence on Input Amplitude:** Unlike linear systems, the response shape (and even the stability) depends on the magnitude of the input signal. A step input of amplitude $1$ may produce a stable response, while a step of amplitude $10$ may drive the system unstable.

- **Generation of New Frequencies:** When a sinusoidal input is applied to a nonlinear system, the steady-state output contains **higher harmonics and sub-harmonics** of the input frequency. For a sinusoidal input $A\sin(\omega t)$, the output may contain components at $\omega, 2\omega, 3\omega, \ldots$ and at $\omega/2, \omega/3, \ldots$.

- **Existence of Limit Cycles:** Nonlinear systems can exhibit self-sustained, periodic oscillations of **fixed amplitude and frequency** even in the absence of external periodic forcing. These are called **limit cycles**, a phenomenon with no counterpart in linear systems.

- **Multiple Equilibrium States (Multiple Singular Points):** A nonlinear system can have more than one equilibrium point, and the system may settle into different equilibria depending on the initial condition.

- **Jump Resonance and Sub-harmonic Oscillations:** Under certain frequency conditions, the system's amplitude response can exhibit sudden "jumps," and sub-harmonic oscillations (oscillations at fractional multiples of the driving frequency) can occur.

- **Bifurcation Behavior:** Small parameter variations can cause qualitative changes in system behavior — creation or annihilation of equilibria, onset of limit cycles, or chaos.

- **Possible Chaotic Behavior:** Some nonlinear systems (e.g., Lorenz system, Duffing oscillator with sufficient forcing) exhibit **deterministic chaos**, characterized by sensitive dependence on initial conditions and bounded but non-periodic trajectories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "list the characteristics of nonlinear systems," always begin with the *failure of the superposition principle* and *input-amplitude dependence*. These two are the most heavily tested points in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers.

### 1.4 Engineering Examples of Nonlinear Systems

A wide range of physical systems exhibit nonlinear behavior:

- **Saturation Amplifier:** Output $y(t) = \text{sat}\{K \cdot u(t)\}$ where the saturation function limits the output to $\pm Y_{\max}$.
- **Dead-Zone Element:** $y(t) = 0$ for $\vert u(t) \vert \leq d$ and $y(t) = K[u(t) - d\,\text{sgn}(u)]$ for $\vert u(t) \vert > d$.
- **Relay (On-Off) Controller:** $y(t) = M \cdot \text{sgn}(u(t))$.
- **Backlash:** Output depends on the history (direction) of input motion.
- **Square-Law Device:** $y(t) = K \cdot u^2(t)$.
- **Pendulum (Large Angle):** Equation $\ddot{\theta} + (g/L)\sin\theta = 0$ — the $\sin\theta$ term is nonlinear, unlike the small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Representation of Nonlinear Systems

A general continuous-time nonlinear dynamical system is described by the state-space representation:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t), t)$$

$$\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t), t)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **state vector** with $n$ states.
- $\mathbf{u}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **input (control) vector**.
- $\mathbf{y}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the **output vector**.
- $\mathbf{f}(\cdot)$ is a **nonlinear vector function** of dimensions $n \times 1$.
- $\mathbf{h}(\cdot)$ is a **nonlinear output function** of dimensions $p \times 1$.

For an **autonomous** nonlinear system (no explicit time dependence and no input):

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t))$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Identifying Nonlinearity

The procedure a KTU examiner expects:

1. **Inspect the governing differential equation** for any term that is a product of variables, a transcendental function (sin, cos, exp, log), or a power higher than 1.
2. **Check for piecewise-defined components** (saturation, dead-zone, relay).
3. **Test the superposition principle** by feeding two inputs and comparing the sum of individual responses to the response to the combined input.
4. **Linearize around an operating point** using Taylor series expansion if the nonlinear region is small.

### 2.3 Classification of Nonlinearities

Nonlinearities in control systems are broadly classified into two categories:

#### A. **Inherent (Natural) Nonlinearities**
These arise from the physical nature of the system components and cannot be separated from the system.

- **Saturation** — occurs in amplifiers, magnetic circuits (when iron saturates), hydraulic valves.
- **Dead-Zone** — caused by static friction (Coulomb friction) in motors, threshold voltages in diodes.
- **Backlash (Hysteresis)** — present in gear trains, mechanical linkages.
- **Squaring and Higher-Order Terms** — fluid flow ($q \propto \sqrt{P}$), drag forces ($F \propto v^2$).
- **Multiplicative Nonlinearity** — effect of one signal on the gain of another (e.g., amplitude-dependent gain).

#### B. **Intentional (Artificial) Nonlinearities**
These are deliberately introduced to improve system performance.

- **Relay Controllers** — used in bang-bang optimal control (e.g., spacecraft attitude control).
- **Nonlinear Damping** — added to suppress overshoot.
- **Variable Structure Controllers** — sliding-mode control.

#### C. **Mathematical Classification**

- **Continuous Nonlinearities** — output is a continuous function of input (e.g., saturation, dead-zone, square law).
- **Discontinuous (Piecewise) Nonlinearities** — output is defined piecewise (e.g., ideal relay, relay with dead-zone).
- **Single-Valued Nonlinearities** — output is a unique function of the current input only (e.g., saturation, dead-zone).
- **Multi-Valued Nonlinearities (Hysteretic)** — output depends on input history (e.g., backlash, hysteresis loops).

### 2.4 State-Space Representation of Nonlinear Systems

A second-order nonlinear system is typically written as:

$$\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_1, x_2)$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_1, x_2)$$

In matrix form for a general $n$-th order nonlinear system:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{x}_n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_1(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \\ f_2(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \\ \vdots \\ f_n(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Example — Simple Pendulum:**
State variables: $x_1 = \theta$ (angle), $x_2 = \dot{\theta}$ (angular velocity).
Equation: $\ddot{\theta} + (g/L)\sin\theta + b\dot{\theta} = 0$

State-space form:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -(g/L)\sin(x_1) - b x_2$$

### 2.5 Phase Plane Analysis

The **phase plane** is a graphical method for studying second-order autonomous systems $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ by plotting $x_2$ versus $x_1$ (state versus state derivative, or one state versus another). The trajectory traced by the system in the $(x_1, x_2)$ plane is called a **phase trajectory** or **phase portrait**.

- **Singular Point (Equilibrium Point):** A point $(x_{1e}, x_{2e})$ where $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ simultaneously. At singular points, the system is at rest.
- **Phase Trajectory:** A curve in the phase plane showing the evolution of system states over time. The direction of motion is indicated by arrows.
- **Iso-cline:** A locus of points where the slope $dx_2/dx_1$ has a constant value.

### 2.6 Stability Definitions in Nonlinear Systems

- **Lyapunov Stability:** An equilibrium point $\mathbf{x}_e$ is stable (in the sense of Lyapunov) if for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that $\Vert \mathbf{x}(0) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert < \delta$ implies $\Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert < \epsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$.

- **Asymptotic Stability:** $\mathbf{x}_e$ is asymptotically stable if it is stable in the sense of Lyapunov **and** $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert = 0$ for all initial conditions within some neighborhood of $\mathbf{x}_e$.

- **Instability:** An equilibrium point $\mathbf{x}_e$ is unstable if it is **not stable**, i.e., there exists an $\epsilon > 0$ for which no $\delta > 0$ can keep the trajectory within the $\epsilon$-ball.

- **Global Asymptotic Stability:** Asymptotic stability valid for **all** initial conditions in the entire state space.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Topic | Formula / Definition | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nonlinearity condition | $N[\alpha u_1 + \beta u_2] \neq \alpha N[u_1] + \beta N[u_2]$ | Failure of superposition |
| General nonlinear state-space | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$ | $\mathbf{f}$ is nonlinear vector field |
| Autonomous form | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ | No explicit $t$ or $\mathbf{u}$ dependence |
| Equilibrium (Singular) point | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e) = \mathbf{0}$ | All state derivatives vanish |
| Phase plane slope | $\dfrac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \dfrac{f_2(x_1, x_2)}{f_1(x_1, x_2)}$ | Valid where $f_1 \neq 0$ |
| Saturation nonlinearity | $y = K u$ for $\vert u \vert \leq a$; $y = \pm Ka$ for $\vert u \vert > a$ | Single-valued continuous |
| Dead-zone nonlinearity | $y = 0$ for $\vert u \vert \leq d$; $y = K(u - d\,\text{sgn}(u))$ | Single-valued continuous |
| Ideal relay | $y = M\,\text{sgn}(u)$ | Discontinuous |
| Lyapunov stability | $\forall \epsilon > 0,\; \exists \delta > 0$ such that $\Vert \mathbf{x}(0) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert < \delta \Rightarrow \Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert < \epsilon,\; \forall t \geq 0$ | Bounded trajectory |
| Asymptotic stability | Lyapunov stable **and** $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert = 0$ | Trajectory returns to equilibrium |
| Instability | $\exists \epsilon > 0$ such that $\forall \delta > 0$, trajectory leaves $\epsilon$-ball | Divergent behavior |

### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Nonlinear control theory is indispensable in modern engineering practice:

- **Aerospace:** Spacecraft attitude control uses **bang-bang (relay) thrusters** for fuel-efficient maneuvers.
- **Robotics:** Manipulator dynamics are inherently nonlinear (Coriolis, centrifugal terms), requiring nonlinear controllers.
- **Power Electronics:** Switching converters (DC-DC buck/boost) operate by **sliding-mode** and **hysteresis** control.
- **Process Control:** pH neutralization and bioreactors exhibit strong nonlinearities requiring gain-scheduling.
- **Automotive:** Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS) and Electronic Stability Programs (ESP) use **nonlinear feedback** to maintain tire grip.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Converting a Second-Order Nonlinear ODE to State-Space Form

**Given System:** A nonlinear mass-spring-damper with a cubic spring stiffness.

$$m\ddot{y} + c\dot{y} + k y + k_3 y^3 = u(t)$$

**Step 1:** Define the state variables. For a second-order system, two state variables are required.

$$x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}$$

This choice ensures first-order derivatives of the state variables can be directly expressed.

**Step 2:** Compute the derivatives of the state variables.

$$\dot{x}_1 = \frac{dy}{dt} = \dot{y} = x_2$$

**Step 3:** Solve the original ODE for the highest-order derivative.

$$\ddot{y} = \frac{1}{m}\left[u(t) - c\dot{y} - k y - k_3 y^3\right]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the state variables.

$$\dot{x}_2 = \ddot{y} = \frac{1}{m}\left[u(t) - c x_2 - k x_1 - k_3 x_1^3\right]$$

**Step 5:** Write the final state-space representation.

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{k}{m}x_1 - \frac{c}{m}x_2 - \frac{k_3}{m}x_1^3 + \frac{1}{m}u(t)$$

In vector form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ -\dfrac{k}{m}x_1 - \dfrac{c}{m}x_2 - \dfrac{k_3}{m}x_1^3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{m} \end{bmatrix} u(t)$$

**Verification of Nonlinearity:** The term $-\dfrac{k_3}{m}x_1^3$ is a cubic function of the state $x_1$, confirming the nonlinear nature of the system.

### 3.2 Derivation: Phase Plane Equation for a Second-Order Autonomous System

**Given System:**

$$\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_1, x_2)$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_1, x_2)$$

**Step 1:** Use the chain rule of calculus.

$$\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{dx_2/dt}{dx_1/dt} = \frac{\dot{x}_2}{\dot{x}_1}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the system equations.

$$\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{f_2(x_1, x_2)}{f_1(x_1, x_2)}$$

**Step 3:** Identify singular points. Singular points occur where both numerators and denominators vanish simultaneously.

$$f_1(x_{1e}, x_{2e}) = 0, \quad f_2(x_{1e}, x_{2e}) = 0$$

At these points, the slope $dx_2/dx_1$ is indeterminate (0/0), and special analysis (linearization) is required to determine the local behavior.

**Step 4:** Apply the chain rule once more to obtain the second-order equation of the phase trajectory.

$$\ddot{x}_1 = \frac{df_1}{dt} = \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1}\dot{x}_1 + \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2}\dot{x}_2$$

This reduced second-order ODE describes the motion of the phase trajectory.

### 3.3 Derivation: Linearization of a Nonlinear System Around an Equilibrium Point

**Given:** Autonomous nonlinear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ with equilibrium $\mathbf{x}_e$ satisfying $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e) = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 1:** Introduce a small perturbation $\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_e$.

**Step 2:** Expand $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e + \delta\mathbf{x})$ using the multivariate Taylor series.

$$\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e + \delta\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e) + \left.\frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\mathbf{x}_e} \delta\mathbf{x} + \text{higher-order terms}$$

**Step 3:** Neglect higher-order terms (valid for small $\delta\mathbf{x}$).

$$\dot{\delta\mathbf{x}} \approx \left.\frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\mathbf{x}_e} \delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A} \delta\mathbf{x}$$

where the **Jacobian matrix** $\mathbf{A}$ is defined as:

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}_{\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_e}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $\mathbf{A}$. The local behavior near $\mathbf{x}_e$ is determined by these eigenvalues:

- **Stable node (asymptotic stability):** Both $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are real and negative.
- **Unstable node (instability):** Both $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are real and positive.
- **Saddle point (instability):** Eigenvalues are real with opposite signs.
- **Stable focus (asymptotic stability):** Complex eigenvalues with negative real parts.
- **Unstable focus (instability):** Complex eigenvalues with positive real parts.
- **Center (Lyapunov stable but not asymptotically stable):** Purely imaginary eigenvalues.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Phase Plane Analysis of Van der Pol Oscillator

**System:** $\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ with $\mu > 0$.

**Step 1:** Convert to state-space form with $x_1 = x, x_2 = \dot{x}$.

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = \mu(1 - x_1^2) x_2 - x_1$$

**Step 2:** Find equilibrium points by setting $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$.

From $\dot{x}_1 = 0$: $x_2 = 0$.

Substituting into $\dot{x}_2 = 0$: $-x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0$.

Thus, the **only equilibrium point** is the origin $(0, 0)$.

**Step 3:** Linearize around $(0, 0)$ by computing the Jacobian.

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_1}{\partial x_2} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix}_{(0,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 - 2\mu x_1 x_2 & \mu(1 - x_1^2) \end{bmatrix}_{(0,0)}$$

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute eigenvalues from $\det(\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$.

$$\det \begin{bmatrix} -\lambda & 1 \\ -1 & \mu - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda - \mu) + 1 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - \mu\lambda + 1 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^2 - 4}}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Interpret results:
- For $0 < \mu < 2$: $\lambda$ are complex with positive real part $\mu/2$, indicating an **unstable focus** at the origin. However, the Van der Pol oscillator has a **stable limit cycle** surrounding this focus.
- For $\mu = 0$: Purely imaginary eigenvalues $\pm j$ — undamped oscillation, a **center**.

**Step 6:** Conclude that the linearization predicts instability at the origin, but the **nonlinear analysis** (using techniques like Poincaré-Bendixson theorem) reveals a **stable limit cycle** of amplitude approximately 2. This is a classic example where linearization alone is insufficient.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Stability Definitions

| Property | Lyapunov Stable | Asymptotically Stable | Unstable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trajectory bounded near equilibrium? | Yes | Yes | No (diverges) |
| Trajectory returns to equilibrium? | Not necessarily | Yes ($\lim_{t \to \infty} = \mathbf{x}_e$) | No |
| Region of attraction | Within $\delta$-ball | Within region of attraction | — |
| Phase portrait example | Closed orbit (center) | Spiral inward (stable focus) | Spiral outward (unstable focus) |
| Linear system equivalent | Marginally stable | Stable (Hurwitz) | Unstable |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: General Nonlinear Control System Architecture

The figure below illustrates a closed-loop nonlinear control system showing where the nonlinearity typically enters the system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    ref[Reference Input R] --> sumA[Comparator Sum Junction]
    sumA --> ctrl[Controller Gc]
    ctrl --> plant[Plant P]
    plant --> nln[Nonlinearity Element N]
    nln --> out[Output Y]
    out --> fb[Feedback Path H]
    fb --> sumA
    fb --> sensor[Sensor F]
    sensor --> fb

    style nln fill:#FFB6C1
    style plant fill:#B0E0E6
    style ctrl fill:#98FB98
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow:** The **Nonlinearity Element N** is shown in red as a critical insertion point in the forward path. In practical systems, the nonlinearity can be embedded inside the plant (inherent) or introduced via the controller (intentional, e.g., relay). The comparator generates the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ which drives the controller, and the feedback path $H(s)$ provides closed-loop regulation.

The following flowchart categorizes nonlinearities as per the KTU 2024 syllabus taxonomy.

```mermaid

    type2 --> relay[Ideal Relay]

```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Phase Plane Construction Procedure

```mermaid
graph LR
    start[Start with second order ODE] --> s1[Step 1 Define state variables x1 and x2]
    s1 --> s2[Step 2 Convert to first order state equations]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3 Find equilibrium points f1 = 0 and f2 = 0]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4 Compute Jacobian Matrix A]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5 Find eigenvalues of A]
    s5 --> s6{Step 6 Classify singular point}
    s6 --> node[Node stable or unstable]
    s6 --> focus[Focus stable or unstable]
    s6 --> saddle[Saddle point]
    s6 --> center[Center]
    s6 --> spiral[Spiral behavior]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Stability Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    stab[Stability of Nonlinear System] --> lyap[Lyapunov Stable]
    stab --> asymp[Asymptotically Stable]
    stab --> inst[Unstable]

    lyap --> lyapDef[Bounded trajectory stays near eq]
    lyap --> centerEx[Example Center Point]

    asymp --> asympDef[Trajectory returns to eq]
    asymp --> globalEx[Global Asymptotic Stability]

    globalEx --> globDef[All initial conditions converge]

    inst --> instDef[Trajectory diverges from eq]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
*List any four basic characteristics of nonlinear systems.*

**Model Answer:**
The four basic characteristics of nonlinear systems are:
1. **Non-applicability of the superposition principle** — the system does not satisfy additivity and homogeneity.
2. **Input-amplitude dependence** — the system response depends on the magnitude of the input.
3. **Generation of new frequencies** — a sinusoidal input produces harmonics and sub-harmonics at the output.
4. **Existence of limit cycles** — self-sustained periodic oscillations of fixed amplitude and frequency without external forcing.

*(Acceptable alternative characteristics: jump resonance, multiple equilibrium points, sub-harmonic oscillations, bifurcation, chaos.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]**
*Define the terms (a) singular point, and (b) phase trajectory in the context of phase plane analysis.*

**Model Answer:**
(a) **Singular Point:** A singular point (or equilibrium point) of an autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ in the phase plane is a point $(x_{1e}, x_{2e})$ at which $\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_{1e}, x_{2e}) = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_{1e}, x_{2e}) = 0$ simultaneously. At such a point, the system is at rest and the phase trajectory is undefined (indeterminate slope).

(b) **Phase Trajectory:** A phase trajectory is the curve traced by the system state $(x_1, x_2)$ in the phase plane as time progresses. The collection of all possible phase trajectories for various initial conditions is called the **phase portrait**, and arrows on the trajectories indicate the direction of increasing time.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** *Explain with suitable examples, the classification of nonlinearities in control systems.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Nonlinearities in control systems are classified based on their physical origin, mathematical nature, and continuity characteristics.

**1. Inherent (Natural) Nonlinearities:**
These arise from the physical laws governing the system components and cannot be removed.
- **Saturation:** Occurs in magnetic circuits (iron-core saturation) and operational amplifiers. For input $u$, the output is $y = Ku$ for $\vert u \vert \leq a$ and $y = \pm Ka$ for $\vert u \vert > a$.
- **Dead-Zone:** Caused by static (Coulomb) friction in motors. The system produces no output for $\vert u \vert \leq d$ and yields $y = K(u - d\,\text{sgn}(u))$ for $\vert u \vert > d$.
- **Backlash:** Present in gear trains where mechanical clearance allows the output to remain stationary while the input reverses.
- **Squaring and higher-order terms:** Drag force in fluid dynamics $F_d \propto v^2$, flow through an orifice $q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$.

**2. Intentional (Artificial) Nonlinearities:**
These are deliberately introduced in the controller to achieve specific performance objectives.
- **Relay (Bang-Bang) Controllers:** Used in time-optimal control of systems with bounded control effort (e.g., thermostat, spacecraft thrusters). $y = M\,\text{sgn}(u)$.
- **Nonlinear Damping:** Inserted to suppress overshoot and improve transient response.

**3. Mathematical Classification:**
- **Continuous Nonlinearities:** Output is a continuous function of input (e.g., saturation, dead-zone, square law).
- **Discontinuous Nonlinearities:** Defined piecewise with possible jumps (e.g., ideal relay).
- **Single-Valued Nonlinearities:** Output depends only on current input (e.g., saturation).
- **Multi-Valued Nonlinearities:** Output depends on input history (e.g., backlash, hysteresis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Note:** Drawing characteristic input-output curves for saturation, dead-zone, and relay is **mandatory** in KTU 2024 ESE answers to secure full 7 marks.

**(b)** *Derive the state-space representation of the nonlinear system described by the differential equation:* **(7 marks)**

$$m\ddot{y} + c\dot{y} + k y + k_3 y^3 = u(t)$$

*Identify the nonlinear term in the representation.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Choose the state variables for a second-order system.

$$x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}$$

**Step 2:** Express the first state derivative.

$$\dot{x}_1 = \dot{y} = x_2$$

**Step 3:** Solve the governing ODE for the second derivative.

$$\ddot{y} = \frac{1}{m}\left[u(t) - c\dot{y} - k y - k_3 y^3\right]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute state variables into the expression for $\ddot{y}$.

$$\dot{x}_2 = \frac{1}{m}\left[u(t) - c x_2 - k x_1 - k_3 x_1^3\right]$$

**Step 5:** Write the complete state-space representation.

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{k}{m}x_1 - \frac{c}{m}x_2 - \frac{k_3}{m}x_1^3 + \frac{1}{m}u(t)$$

In compact vector form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\dfrac{k}{m} & -\dfrac{c}{m} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -\dfrac{k_3}{m}x_1^3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{m} \end{bmatrix} u(t)$$

**Step 6:** Identify the nonlinear term.

The nonlinear term is $-\dfrac{k_3}{m}x_1^3$, which is a **cubic function of state $x_1$**. This is also known as a **Duffing-type nonlinearity** and is responsible for the nonlinear behavior of the system.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Define asymptotic stability and instability of nonlinear systems. Explain the difference between local and global asymptotic stability.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Asymptotic Stability:**
An equilibrium point $\mathbf{x}_e$ of the system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ is **asymptotically stable** if:
1. It is stable in the sense of Lyapunov, **AND**
2. $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert = 0$ for all initial conditions $\mathbf{x}(0)$ within some neighborhood of $\mathbf{x}_e$ called the **region of attraction** or **domain of attraction**.

In the phase plane, asymptotically stable equilibria appear as **stable nodes** (trajectories approach along straight-line directions) or **stable foci/spirals** (trajectories spiral inward).

**Instability:**
An equilibrium point $\mathbf{x}_e$ is **unstable** if it is not stable in the sense of Lyapunov. Formally, there exists an $\epsilon > 0$ such that for any $\delta > 0$, there exists an initial condition $\mathbf{x}(0)$ with $\Vert \mathbf{x}(0) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert < \delta$ but $\Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_e \Vert \geq \epsilon$ for some $t > 0$.

In the phase plane, unstable equilibria appear as **unstable nodes**, **unstable foci**, or **saddle points**.

**Local vs. Global Asymptotic Stability:**
- **Local Asymptotic Stability:** Asymptotic stability holds only for initial conditions within a finite **region of attraction** $D \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ surrounding $\mathbf{x}_e$. Outside this region, trajectories may diverge or converge to a different equilibrium.
- **Global Asymptotic Stability:** Asymptotic stability holds for **all** initial conditions in the entire state space $\mathbb{R}^n$, i.e., the region of attraction is the entire state space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For the simple pendulum with friction, the downward equilibrium $\theta = 0$ is **locally** asymptotically stable (for moderate initial angles). For the system $\dot{x} = -x^3$, the origin is **globally** asymptotically stable because $V(x) = x^2/2$ serves as a Lyapunov function for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

**(b)** *Consider the nonlinear system:* **(7 marks)**

$$\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2^2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_2$$

*Find all singular points and classify them using the Jacobian method. Sketch the phase portrait qualitatively.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find equilibrium points.
Set $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$:

$$-x_1 + x_2^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = x_2^2$$

$$-x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 0$$

Substituting $x_2 = 0$ into the first equation: $x_1 = 0$.

**The only equilibrium point is the origin $(0, 0)$.**

**Step 2:** Compute the Jacobian matrix.

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_1}{\partial x_2} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial \dot{x}_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 2x_2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

At the origin $(0, 0)$:

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute eigenvalues.

$$\det(\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = \det \begin{bmatrix} -1 - \lambda & 0 \\ 0 & -1 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = (-1 - \lambda)^2 = 0$$

$$\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = -1$$

**Step 4:** Classify the singular point.

Both eigenvalues are **real, negative, and repeated** ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = -1$). Check the eigenvectors:

$$(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

This yields **two linearly independent eigenvectors**, so the origin is a **Stable Node (Asymptotically Stable)**.

**Step 5:** Qualitative phase portrait.
- All trajectories in the neighborhood of the origin approach the origin along well-defined linear directions.
- Far from the origin, the $x_2^2$ term dominates in $\dot{x}_1$, causing curved trajectories.
- The system is asymptotically stable at the origin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Misidentifying singular points:** Always verify that BOTH state derivatives are zero at a singular point. KTU examiners frequently test systems with **multiple equilibria** (e.g., $\dot{x} = x - x^3$) to check whether students can find all of them.
> 2. **Forgetting the nonlinear term when linearizing:** The Taylor series expansion must be computed at the equilibrium point with **all partial derivatives evaluated at that point**. Substituting equilibrium values *after* computing derivatives is the safe approach.
> 3. **Conflating "Lyapunov stable" with "asymptotically stable":** A center (e.g., undamped pendulum) is Lyapunov stable but NOT asymptotically stable. KTU marks are deducted for not distinguishing these.
> 4. **Improperly drawing input-output characteristic curves:** When asked to "explain" a nonlinearity (saturation, dead-zone), you must draw the curve with labeled axes and regions. Text-only answers lose 2-3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Superposition principle failure** is the defining property of any nonlinear system.
- **Input-amplitude dependence** means linear analysis tools (Bode, Nyquist, root locus) are generally invalid.
- Nonlinear systems can exhibit **limit cycles** (Van der Pol), **chaos** (Lorenz), and **bifurcations** — none of which exist in linear systems.
- The **state-space form** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$ unifies the description of linear and nonlinear systems.
- **Classification of nonlinearities:** Inherent vs. Intentional; Continuous vs. Discontinuous; Single-valued vs. Multi-valued.
- **Phase plane analysis** is a graphical method restricted to second-order systems, plotting $x_2$ vs. $x_1$.
- **Singular points** are equilibria where $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e) = \mathbf{0}$; classified as nodes, foci, saddle points, or centers.
- **Local behavior** near a singular point is determined by the **eigenvalues of the Jacobian matrix** at that point.
- **Lyapunov stable** = trajectory stays near equilibrium. **Asymptotically stable** = trajectory returns to equilibrium. **Unstable** = trajectory diverges.
- **Global asymptotic stability** requires convergence from ALL initial conditions in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Two marks** are usually awarded in KTU ESE for **drawing the characteristic input-output curves** of named nonlinearities — never skip them.
- The **Jacobian method** for linearization is a high-yield KTU topic — practice the partial derivative computation rigorously.
- For a second-order system with **eigenvalue summary**: Real & negative → stable node; real & positive → unstable node; real & opposite signs → saddle; complex with negative real part → stable focus; complex with positive real part → unstable focus; purely imaginary → center.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction Using Isocline Method and Classification of Equilibrium Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET752 - NONLINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Construction Using Isocline Method and Classification of Equilibrium Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Isocline Method — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Isocline):** An *isocline* is the locus of all points in the phase plane along which the trajectories of a second-order autonomous system have a **constant slope**. It is the curve where the tangent to every phase trajectory makes a fixed angle with the horizontal axis.

Consider a general second-order autonomous (time-invariant) nonlinear system of the form:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_1, x_2)
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_1, x_2)
$$

The slope of the phase trajectory in the $(x_1, x_2)$ plane is given by the differential relation:

$$
\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{f_2(x_1, x_2)}{f_1(x_1, x_2)}
$$

The **isocline equation** corresponding to a chosen constant slope $\alpha$ is therefore:

$$
\frac{f_2(x_1, x_2)}{f_1(x_1, x_2)} = \alpha
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The isocline method is the classical *graphical construction technique* for plotting the phase portrait of second-order nonlinear systems. It is the geometric analog of the "direction field" used in ODE textbooks and is heavily tested in KTU ESE Module 1 questions.

## 1.2 Classification of Equilibrium Points — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Equilibrium / Singular Point):** An *equilibrium point* (also called a *critical point*, *singular point*, or *stationary point*) of the autonomous system is any point $(x_{1e}, x_{2e})$ in the state space where both state derivatives simultaneously vanish: $f_1(x_{1e}, x_{2e}) = 0$ and $f_2(x_{1e}, x_{2e}) = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Classification):** *Classification* of equilibrium points is the procedure of determining the *qualitative* local behavior of trajectories in a small neighborhood of an equilibrium point. The classification is performed by **linearizing** the nonlinear system at the equilibrium point using the Jacobian matrix and inspecting the **eigenvalues** of the resulting linear system.

The six canonical classifications that appear in KTU examinations are:

| Class | Eigenvalue Pattern | Stability |
|---|---|---|
| Stable Node | Real, distinct, both **negative** | Asymptotically stable |
| Unstable Node | Real, distinct, both **positive** | Unstable |
| Saddle Point | Real, **opposite** signs | Unstable |
| Stable Focus (Spiral) | Complex, $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$ | Asymptotically stable |
| Unstable Focus (Spiral) | Complex, $\text{Re}(\lambda) > 0$ | Unstable |
| Center (Vortex) | Purely imaginary, $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$ | Lyapunov stable (not asymptotically) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The River Map

Imagine you are standing on the bank of a wide, slow-moving river. The water is the **state trajectory** of the system, and the local **velocity vector** of the water at every point is the **vector field** $(\dot{x}_1, \dot{x}_2)$.

- An **isocline** is like marking on the riverbed the locus of points where the current flows at exactly the **same compass heading** (say, $30^\circ$ NE). If you draw enough of these "same-direction" curves, you can then sketch the streamline of the water by drawing arrows that smoothly change from one heading to the next.
- An **equilibrium point** is the location of a *drain* or a *spring* — a place where the water neither moves in nor out. By studying the immediate surroundings of the drain (does water spiral in? Does it shoot away? Does it hover in circles?), you **classify** the point.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** The name *iso-cline* literally means *equal slope* (Greek *isos* = equal, *klinein* = to slope). Whenever you see "iso-" in engineering, think of a *locus of constant property*.

## 1.4 Physical / Engineering Constants

- **Slope parameter $\alpha$** (dimensionless, real number, $\alpha \in \mathbb{R} \cup \{\infty\}$): the assigned tangent slope on an isocline. The special isocline $\alpha = 0$ is the locus $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ and $\alpha = \infty$ is the locus $\dot{x}_1 = 0$.
- **Jacobian matrix $J$** (entries in units of $1/\text{time}$): local linearization operator.
- **Eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2}$** (units of $1/\text{time}$): natural frequencies of the linearized dynamics.
- **Trace** $\tau = \text{tr}(J) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$ (units of $1/\text{time}$).
- **Determinant** $\Delta = \det(J) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2$ (units of $1/\text{time}^2$).
- **Discriminant** $D = \tau^2 - 4\Delta$ (units of $1/\text{time}^2$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Isocline Construction

The isocline method is a **graphical direction-field technique**. The procedure is universal and applies to *any* second-order autonomous system.

**Step 1 — Identify the autonomous ODEs.** Write the system in the canonical form $\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_1,x_2)$ and $\dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_1,x_2)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the slope function.** Form the ratio:

$$
S(x_1, x_2) = \frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{f_2(x_1, x_2)}{f_1(x_1, x_2)}
$$

**Step 3 — Select a discrete set of slope values.** Choose a family of constants $\alpha_k$, typically in equal increments. A standard KTU choice is $\alpha \in \{0, \pm 1/3, \pm 1, \pm 3, \infty\}$, giving nine isoclines that fully define the qualitative portrait. The choice $\alpha = 0$ gives a horizontal-tangent isocline; $\alpha = \infty$ gives a vertical-tangent isocline.

**Step 4 — Plot each isocline.** For each $\alpha_k$, draw the curve $f_2(x_1, x_2) = \alpha_k \cdot f_1(x_1, x_2)$ in the phase plane. This is the *locus of constant slope* $\alpha_k$.

**Step 5 — Annotate the direction field.** At several points along each isocline, draw a small line segment with the prescribed slope $\alpha_k$. These short tangent segments form the **direction field** of the system.

**Step 6 — Synthesize trajectories.** Starting from any initial condition, draw a smooth curve whose local tangent always matches the direction-field segment at that location. Continue until the trajectory reaches a known feature (equilibrium, infinity, or another well-defined isocline).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** The isocline method converts the *pointwise* information (vector field) into a *global* information (the curve) of constant slope. By interpolating between adjacent isoclines, the construction approximates the integral curve of the system — the phase trajectory.

## 2.2 Special Isoclines of Particular Interest

| Isocline | Equation | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha = 0$ | $f_2(x_1, x_2) = 0$ | Trajectories are **horizontal** |
| $\alpha = \infty$ | $f_1(x_1, x_2) = 0$ | Trajectories are **vertical** |
| $\alpha = 1$ | $f_2 = f_1$ | Trajectories are at $45^\circ$ |
| $\alpha = -1$ | $f_2 = -f_1$ | Trajectories are at $-45^\circ$ |
| $\alpha = \frac{f_2}{f_1}$ | Defines the trajectory itself | This is **not** drawn as an isocline but reveals the actual path |

## 2.3 Classification of Equilibrium Points — The Linearization Recipe

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hartman–Grobman Theorem (1960):** *Near a hyperbolic equilibrium point (no eigenvalue with zero real part), the qualitative behavior of a nonlinear system is topologically equivalent to that of its linearization.* This theorem is what **justifies** the entire classification procedure used in KTU examinations.

**Step 1 — Find the equilibrium point(s).** Solve $f_1 = 0$ and $f_2 = 0$ simultaneously.

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian matrix.** 

$$
J(x_1,x_2) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $J$ at the equilibrium point.**

**Step 4 — Compute the characteristic equation.** 

$$
\lambda^2 - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0
$$

where $\tau = \text{tr}(J)$ and $\Delta = \det(J)$.

**Step 5 — Compute eigenvalues.** 

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the classification table (Section 1.2).**

**Step 7 — (Optional) Compute eigenvectors to find the *invariant axes* (separatrices) of nodes and saddle points.** The eigenvectors give the *principal directions* along which the linearized dynamics are decoupled.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Valuation:** Always quote eigenvalues in **two equivalent forms** — both the (real/complex) and the (trace, determinant) forms. Examiners award 1–2 marks for correctly identifying *both* representations.

$$
\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} \;=\; \frac{f_2(x_1, x_2)}{f_1(x_1, x_2)} \;=\; \alpha
$$

$$
f_2(x_1, x_2) \;=\; \alpha \cdot f_1(x_1, x_2)
$$

$$
J \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \partial f_1/\partial x_1 & \partial f_1/\partial x_2 \\ \partial f_2/\partial x_1 & \partial f_2/\partial x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - \tau \lambda + \Delta \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\lambda_{1,2} \;=\; \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}
$$

$$
D \;=\; \tau^2 - 4\Delta
$$

| **Parameter** | **Condition** | **Classification** | **Stability** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta < 0$ | Eigenvalues real & of opposite sign | **Saddle Point** | Unstable |
| $\Delta > 0, \; \tau > 0, \; D > 0$ | Real, both positive | **Unstable Node** | Unstable |
| $\Delta > 0, \; \tau < 0, \; D > 0$ | Real, both negative | **Stable Node** | Asymptotically Stable |
| $\Delta > 0, \; \tau > 0, \; D < 0$ | Complex, $\text{Re}(\lambda) > 0$ | **Unstable Focus / Spiral** | Unstable |
| $\Delta > 0, \; \tau < 0, \; D < 0$ | Complex, $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$ | **Stable Focus / Spiral** | Asymptotically Stable |
| $\Delta > 0, \; \tau = 0, \; D < 0$ | Purely imaginary | **Center / Vortex** | Lyapunov Stable |
| $\Delta = 0$ | At least one zero eigenvalue | **Non-isolated / Line of Equilibria** | Not classifiable by linearization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Sub-Cases (Repeated Eigenvalues):** When $D = 0$ and $\Delta > 0$, we have a *repeated* real eigenvalue $\lambda = \tau/2$. Sub-cases:
> - **Star Node:** Two independent eigenvectors (only when $J$ is a scalar multiple of identity). Trajectories approach/leave along radial lines.
> - **Degenerate Node:** Only one linearly independent eigenvector. Trajectories approach along a *tangent direction* and then curve toward the eigenvector.
> - **Improper Node:** Trajectories approach along one direction and approach infinity in another.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The isocline method and equilibrium classification underpin the design and analysis of:

- **Pendulum and robotic-arm control:** Classifying the upright (saddle) and downward (center / stable focus with damping) equilibria.
- **DC motor drives:** Identifying stable operating points of armature-controlled and field-controlled motors.
- **Power system stability:** Swing-equation equilibria and their stability margins — the famous *equal-area criterion* uses the saddle-point classification.
- **Aircraft dynamics:** Longitudinal and lateral mode equilibria (phugoid, short-period, Dutch roll).
- **Phase-locked loops and oscillators:** Center and limit-cycle analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Note:** In MATLAB/Simulink, the **PPLANE** and **DFIELD** tools (developed at Harvard) are direct computational implementations of the isocline method. KTU lab examinations often expect students to know these tools conceptually.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Isocline Equation from the Phase Trajectory

We start with a second-order autonomous nonlinear system:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_1, x_2) \quad \quad \dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_1, x_2)
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the chain rule.** By the chain rule of calculus, the slope of the phase trajectory is:

$$
\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{dx_2/dt}{dx_1/dt} = \frac{\dot{x}_2}{\dot{x}_1} = \frac{f_2(x_1, x_2)}{f_1(x_1, x_2)}
$$

This is a single first-order ODE for the trajectory in the $(x_1, x_2)$ plane, valid everywhere except on the locus $f_1 = 0$ (where the slope is undefined / vertical).

**Step 2 — Define the isocline.** We *constrain* the slope to be a constant $\alpha$:

$$
\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{f_2(x_1, x_2)}{f_1(x_1, x_2)} = \alpha
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply to obtain the explicit locus.** Rearranging:

$$
f_2(x_1, x_2) = \alpha \cdot f_1(x_1, x_2)
$$

This single scalar equation defines the $\alpha$-isocline — the curve of constant trajectory slope.

**Step 4 — Verify with the linear system.** Consider the linear system $\dot{x}_1 = ax_1 + bx_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = cx_1 + dx_2$. The $\alpha$-isocline satisfies:

$$
cx_1 + dx_2 = \alpha(ax_1 + bx_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad (c - \alpha a) x_1 + (d - \alpha b) x_2 = 0
$$

This is a **family of straight lines through the origin** with slope $\frac{\alpha a - c}{d - \alpha b}$ (assuming non-degenerate). For a real $2\times 2$ system, there is therefore a one-to-one correspondence between the slope $\alpha$ and the orientation of the corresponding straight-line isocline.

## 3.2 Derivation: Eigenvalue Classification from the Jacobian

We linearize the system around an equilibrium point $(x_{1e}, x_{2e})$. Let $\delta x_1 = x_1 - x_{1e}$ and $\delta x_2 = x_2 - x_{2e}$. A first-order Taylor expansion of $f_1$ and $f_2$ gives:

$$
\delta \dot{x}_1 = \left.\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1}\right|_e \delta x_1 + \left.\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2}\right|_e \delta x_2
$$

$$
\delta \dot{x}_2 = \left.\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1}\right|_e \delta x_1 + \left.\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2}\right|_e \delta x_2
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \delta \dot{x}_1 \\ \delta \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = J \begin{bmatrix} \delta x_1 \\ \delta x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $J$ is the Jacobian evaluated at the equilibrium. Assuming solutions of the form $\delta x_i = K_i e^{\lambda t}$, we obtain the eigenvalue problem:

$$
\det(J - \lambda I) = 0
$$

Expanding the determinant:

$$
(J_{11} - \lambda)(J_{22} - \lambda) - J_{12} J_{21} = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - (J_{11} + J_{22}) \lambda + (J_{11} J_{22} - J_{12} J_{21}) = 0
$$

Identifying the trace and determinant:

$$
\lambda^2 - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0
$$

with $\tau = J_{11} + J_{22}$ and $\Delta = J_{11} J_{22} - J_{12} J_{21}$. The discriminant is:

$$
D = \tau^2 - 4\Delta
$$

The closed-form eigenvalues are:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{D}}{2}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Van der Pol System (KTU Style)

**Problem.** Consider the Van der Pol oscillator:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2 - (x_1^2 - 1) x_1
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -x_1
$$

**(a) Find all equilibrium points.**

Setting $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$:

$$
-x_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 0
$$

$$
x_2 - (0 - 1) \cdot 0 = x_2 = 0
$$

The system has a **unique** equilibrium point at the origin: $(0, 0)$.

**(b) Compute the Jacobian and its eigenvalues.**

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} \partial f_1/\partial x_1 & \partial f_1/\partial x_2 \\ \partial f_2/\partial x_1 & \partial f_2/\partial x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 3x_1^2 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

At $(0,0)$:

$$
J\big|_{(0,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Trace: $\tau = 1 + 0 = 1$. Determinant: $\Delta = (1)(0) - (1)(-1) = 1$. Discriminant:

$$
D = \tau^2 - 4\Delta = 1 - 4 = -3
$$

Since $\Delta > 0$, $D < 0$, and $\tau > 0$, the equilibrium is an **Unstable Focus / Spiral**.

Eigenvalues:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{1 \pm j\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$

**Verification by the trace-determinant rule:** $\Delta > 0$ rules out saddle; $D < 0$ rules out nodes (real eigenvalues); $\tau > 0$ rules out stable focus and center. Result: Unstable Focus. ✓

**(c) Construct the isocline $\alpha = -1$.**

The isocline equation $f_2 = \alpha f_1$ becomes:

$$
-x_1 = -1 \cdot \bigl[x_2 - (x_1^2 - 1) x_1\bigr]
$$

Expanding:

$$
-x_1 = -x_2 + (x_1^2 - 1) x_1
$$

$$
-x_1 = -x_2 + x_1^3 - x_1
$$

$$
x_2 = x_1^3
$$

So the $\alpha = -1$ isocline is the **cubic curve** $x_2 = x_1^3$. All trajectories crossing this curve have local slope $-1$ (i.e., descending at $45^\circ$).

**(d) Construct the isocline $\alpha = 0$.**

The isocline equation is $f_2 = 0$:

$$
-x_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 0
$$

This is the **vertical $x_2$-axis**. Trajectories crossing it are **horizontal**.

**(e) Construct the isocline $\alpha = \infty$.**

The isocline equation is $f_1 = 0$:

$$
x_2 = (x_1^2 - 1) x_1 = x_1^3 - x_1
$$

This is the **cubic curve** $x_2 = x_1^3 - x_1$, passing through $(0,0)$, $(\pm 1, 0)$. Trajectories crossing it are **vertical**.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Pendulum Classification

**Problem.** The undamped pendulum in state-space form is:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = -\frac{g}{\ell} \sin x_1
$$

**(a) Equilibria.** $x_2 = 0$ and $\sin x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = n\pi$ for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. Equilibria: $(n\pi, 0)$.

**(b) Jacobian.**

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{g}{\ell}\cos x_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

At $x_1 = 0$ (downward equilibrium): $J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -g/\ell & 0 \end{bmatrix}$. Trace $\tau = 0$, Determinant $\Delta = g/\ell > 0$, $D = -4g/\ell < 0$. Eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2} = \pm j\sqrt{g/\ell}$. **Center** (Lyapunov stable).

At $x_1 = \pi$ (upward equilibrium): $J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ +g/\ell & 0 \end{bmatrix}$. Trace $\tau = 0$, Determinant $\Delta = -g/\ell < 0$. **Saddle point** (unstable).

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The downward equilibrium of the pendulum is a **center** (oscillatory, neutrally stable for the *ideal* undamped case). The upward (inverted) equilibrium is a **saddle** — an unstable equilibrium, which is why balancing a broomstick on your palm is so difficult!

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Eigenvalue ↔ Geometry Cross-Reference

| **Eigenvalue** | **Trajectory Shape near Equilibrium** | **Time Response** |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda_1 < 0, \, \lambda_2 < 0$ (distinct) | **Stable Node** — all trajectories enter monotonically along two principal axes | $c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}$ decays |
| $\lambda_1 > 0, \, \lambda_2 > 0$ (distinct) | **Unstable Node** — all trajectories leave monotonically | Pure exponential growth |
| $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 < 0$ | **Saddle** — trajectories approach along stable manifold, leave along unstable manifold | $c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}$, mixed signs |
| $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$, complex | **Stable Focus / Spiral** — inward spiraling | Decaying sinusoid: $e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t)$ |
| $\text{Re}(\lambda) > 0$, complex | **Unstable Focus / Spiral** — outward spiraling | Growing sinusoid |
| $\lambda = \pm j\omega_n$ | **Center / Vortex** — closed elliptical orbits | Pure sinusoid: $\sin(\omega_n t)$ |
| $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 < 0$, 2 eigenvectors | **Stable Star Node** — radial approach from all directions | $(c_1 + c_2 t) e^{\lambda t}$ |
| $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 < 0$, 1 eigenvector | **Stable Degenerate Node** — tangent approach then eigenvector approach | $(c_1 + c_2 t) e^{\lambda t}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Isocline Method Construction Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Autonomous 2nd-Order System dot-x1 = f1, dot-x2 = f2"] --> B["Form slope function S = f2 divided by f1"]
    B --> C["Select discrete slope values alpha-k = 0, 0.333, 1, 3, infinity"]
    C --> D["For each alpha-k, draw isocline curve f2 = alpha-k times f1"]
    D --> E["Annotate each isocline with short tangent segments of slope alpha-k"]
    E --> F["Construct direction field by interpolating between isoclines"]
    F --> G["Draw smooth phase trajectory following local tangents"]
    G --> H{"Does trajectory reach an equilibrium point?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Mark Equilibrium, Classify via Jacobian Eigenvalues"]
    H -- No --> J["Continue to boundary or to infinity"]
    I --> K["Complete Phase Portrait"]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Nonlinear System f1, f2"] --> B["Find Equilibria: Solve f1 = 0 AND f2 = 0"]
    B --> C["Compute Jacobian Matrix J at each equilibrium"]
    C --> D["Compute Trace tau and Determinant Delta"]
    D --> E{"Delta less than 0?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["SADDLE POINT - Unstable"]
    E -- No --> G{"D = tau squared minus 4 Delta"}
    G --> H{"D less than 0?"}
    H -- Yes --> I{"tau less than 0?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["STABLE FOCUS / SPIRAL"]
    I -- No --> K["UNSTABLE FOCUS / SPIRAL"]
    H -- No --> L{"tau less than 0?"}
    L -- Yes --> M["STABLE NODE"]
    L -- No --> N["UNSTABLE NODE"]
    G --> O{"tau equals 0?"}
    O -- Yes --> P["CENTER - Lyapunov Stable"]
    G --> Q{"D equals 0?"}
    Q -- Yes --> R["DEGENERATE or STAR NODE - Inspect Eigenvectors"]
```

## 4.3 State Diagram: Equilibrium Point Topology

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> System
    System --> Hyperbolic: Both eigenvalues with non-zero real part
    System --> NonHyperbolic: At least one eigenvalue on imaginary axis
    Hyperbolic --> StableNode: Real, both negative
    Hyperbolic --> UnstableNode: Real, both positive
    Hyperbolic --> SaddlePoint: Real, opposite signs
    Hyperbolic --> StableFocus: Complex, Re less than 0
    Hyperbolic --> UnstableFocus: Complex, Re greater than 0
    NonHyperbolic --> Center: Purely imaginary pair
    NonHyperbolic --> HigherOrder: Degenerate cases
    StableNode --> AsymptoticStable
    StableFocus --> AsymptoticStable
    Center --> LyapunovStable
    UnstableNode --> Unstable
    UnstableFocus --> Unstable
    SaddlePoint --> Unstable
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic: Trace-Determinant Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Plane["TRACE-DETERMINANT PLANE"]
        Origin["Origin: tau = 0, Delta = 0"]
        Saddle["Saddle Region: Delta less than 0"]
        Center["Center: tau = 0, Delta greater than 0"]
        StableNode["Stable Node: tau less than 0, Delta greater than 0, D greater than 0"]
        UnstableNode["Unstable Node: tau greater than 0, Delta greater than 0, D greater than 0"]
        StableFocus["Stable Spiral: tau less than 0, D less than 0"]
        UnstableFocus["Unstable Spiral: tau greater than 0, D less than 0"]
    end
    Origin -.-> Center
    Saddle --- StableNode
    Saddle --- UnstableNode
    StableNode --- StableFocus
    UnstableNode --- UnstableFocus
```
