## 4.3 Backstepping — Recursive Design Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Step 1: Define z1 = x1 - x1d] --> B[Choose alpha1 with c1 greater than 0]
    B --> C[Define z2 = x2 - alpha1]
    C --> D[Augment V1 to V2 = 0.5 z1 squared + 0.5 z2 squared]
    D --> E[Compute dV2 dz2 and design u]
    E --> F[Final Control u with c2 greater than 0]
    F --> G[Stability: dV2 equals -c1 z1 squared - c2 z2 squared]
    G --> H{System Order n greater than 2}
    H -- yes --> I[Repeat Steps for z3 to zn]
    H -- no --> J[End: Global Asymptotic Stability]

```

**Interpretation for KTU answers:** Each "Step" in the flowchart corresponds to *one backstepping recursion*. After the $k$-th step, $V_k$ is a Lyapunov function for the closed-loop sub-system $(z_1,\dots,z_k)$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define *sliding mode control*. State the role of the **sliding surface** and the **reaching condition**.

**Model Answer:**
Sliding Mode Control (SMC) is a variable-structure control in which the control input is *switched* discontinuously about a predefined **sliding surface** $s(x)=0$ in the state space.
- The **sliding surface** $s(x) = c^{T}(x - x_d)$ is a manifold whose dynamics on $s=0$ are designed to be Hurwitz (eigenvalues stable), so that the reduced-order motion converges to the origin.
- The **reaching condition** $s\,\dot{s} < 0$ for all $s \neq 0$ (typically enforced as $s\,\dot{s} \le -\eta\vert s\vert$, $\eta>0$) guarantees that every trajectory outside the surface is driven *toward* it in finite time.
- Once on $s=0$, the closed-loop dynamics become **invariant** to matched parameter variations and disturbances — the central robustness property of SMC.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is **gain scheduling**? Mention its two main limitations.

**Model Answer:**
Gain scheduling is a nonlinear control technique in which the controller gains are varied as a function of measurable *scheduling variables* (e.g., speed, altitude) by interpolating between a bank of linear controllers designed at several operating points.
**Limitations:**
1. **No global stability guarantee** — local linearizations are valid only near operating points, and stability is lost in fast transients or if the scheduling variable changes too rapidly (failure of the frozen-gain principle).
2. **Limited robustness** — gain scheduling has no inherent disturbance rejection beyond what each individual linear loop provides (unlike sliding mode, which is invariant to matched disturbances).
3. (Extra KTU point) Implementation complexity: requires careful *bumpless transfer* and *anti-windup* design to avoid switching transients.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Sliding Mode Control (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**For the second-order nonlinear plant**
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -2x_1 - x_2^{2}\sin(x_1) + (1 + x_1^{2})\,u + d(t)$$
**where $\vert d(t) \vert \le 5$ is a matched disturbance:**

#### (a) Design a sliding mode controller to track a smooth reference $x_{1d}(t)$. Derive the equivalent control $u_{eq}$, the switching control, and prove the reaching condition via a Lyapunov function. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the error and the sliding surface.**
Let $e = x_1 - x_{1d}$ and $\dot{e} = x_2 - \dot{x}_{1d}$. Choose a Hurwitz sliding variable
$$s = \dot{e} + \lambda e = (x_2 - \dot{x}_{1d}) + \lambda(x_1 - x_{1d}), \quad \lambda > 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\dot{s}$ and isolate $u$.**
$$\dot{s} = \ddot{e} + \lambda\dot{e} = \dot{x}_2 - \ddot{x}_{1d} + \lambda(x_2 - \dot{x}_{1d})$$
Substituting $\dot{x}_2$:
$$\dot{s} = -2x_1 - x_2^{2}\sin(x_1) + (1 + x_1^{2})u + d(t) - \ddot{x}_{1d} + \lambda(x_2 - \dot{x}_{1d})$$
Group known terms as $F(x,t) = -2x_1 - x_2^{2}\sin(x_1) - \ddot{x}_{1d} + \lambda(x_2 - \dot{x}_{1d})$:
$$\dot{s} = F + (1 + x_1^{2})u + d(t)$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent control** (set $\dot{s}=0$ and ignore $d$):
$$u_{eq} = -\frac{F(x,t)}{1 + x_1^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Switching control** (to enforce $\dot{s} = -K\,\text{sgn}(s)$):
$$u = u_{eq} - \frac{K}{1 + x_1^{2}}\,\text{sgn}(s), \quad K > 0$$
With $g(x) = 1 + x_1^{2} > 0$ everywhere, this is well-defined.

**Step 5 — Reach and stability proof.** Substitute:
$$\dot{s} = F + (1 + x_1^{2})\!\left[-\frac{F}{1 + x_1^{2}} - \frac{K}{1 + x_1^{2}}\,\text{sgn}(s)\right] + d = -K\,\text{sgn}(s) + d(t)$$

**Step 6 — Lyapunov.** $V = \tfrac{1}{2}s^{2}$:
$$\dot{V} = s\dot{s} = -K\vert s\vert + s\,d(t) \le -(K - 5)\vert s\vert$$
Choose $K > 5 + \eta$ with $\eta>0$. Then $\dot{V} \le -\eta \vert s \vert \le 0$ for all $s \neq 0$. Finite-time reach follows from $\dot{V} \le -\eta\sqrt{2V}$.

Once on $s=0$, the dynamics reduce to $\dot{e} + \lambda e = 0$, giving exponential convergence of $e \to 0$ at rate $\lambda$.

#### (b) Explain the *chattering* phenomenon in SMC. Discuss **two chattering-reduction techniques** and their limitations. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Chattering Definition:** Chattering is the high-frequency oscillation of the control input and state trajectory around the sliding surface $s=0$, caused by the discontinuous $\text{sgn}(s)$ term combined with finite actuator bandwidth and unmodelled high-frequency dynamics.

**Cause:** Real systems have *unmodelled fast dynamics* (actuator lag, sample-and-hold, structural modes). The discontinuous control excites these modes, and the resulting delays prevent exact sliding — the system chatters inside a boundary layer.

**Technique 1 — Boundary-Layer Saturation Function.** Replace $\text{sgn}(s)$ with $\text{sat}(s/\Phi)$ where $\text{sat}(y) = y$ for $\vert y \vert \le 1$ and $\text{sgn}(y)$ for $\vert y \vert > 1$. The boundary-layer width $\Phi$ trades off *chattering suppression* (larger $\Phi$) against *tracking accuracy / robustness* (smaller $\Phi$). The system *no longer* has exact invariance — a steady-state error $\vert s \vert \le \Phi$ persists.

**Technique 2 — Higher-Order Sliding Modes (HOSM), e.g., Twisting or Super-Twisting.** The control is designed to constrain $s$, $\dot{s}$, …, $s^{(r-1)} = 0$ simultaneously, producing a *continuous* $u$ with finite-time convergence. The Twisting algorithm is $u = -K_1\,\text{sgn}(s) - K_2\,\text{sgn}(\dot{s})$ with $K_1 > K_2 > 0$ chosen to ensure convergence. Limitation: requires *measurement of $\dot{s}$* (often done via Levant's differentiator) and higher gain selection effort.

**Other techniques (extra KTU points):** observer-based chattering suppression, fuzzy / neural smoothing of the discontinuous term, and equivalent-control-averaging (hysteresis modulation).

---

### Question B — Backstepping & Tracking (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**Consider the strict-feedback system**
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_1^{2} + x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = (2 + \cos x_1)\,u$$
**with the tracking objective $x_1 \to x_{1d}(t) = \sin(t)$.**

#### (a) Apply the **backstepping procedure** to design a stabilizing controller. Provide the virtual control $\alpha_1$ and the final control $u$. Prove stability with an explicit composite Lyapunov function. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define tracking error and compute its derivative.**
$$z_1 = x_1 - x_{1d}, \quad \dot{z}_1 = x_1^{2} + x_2 - \dot{x}_{1d} = x_1^{2} + x_2 - \cos(t)$$
Note that $x_1^{2}$ is a *known nonlinearity in $z_1$ alone* — this is the strict-feedback structure.

**Step 2 — Design virtual control $\alpha_1$ to stabilize the $z_1$-subsystem.**
$$\alpha_1 = -c_1 z_1 - x_1^{2} + \dot{x}_{1d} = -c_1 z_1 - x_1^{2} + \cos(t), \quad c_1 > 0$$
With $z_2 = x_2 - \alpha_1$:
$$\dot{z}_1 = -c_1 z_1 + z_2$$
First Lyapunov function $V_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}z_1^{2}$:
$$\dot{V}_1 = z_1 \dot{z}_1 = -c_1 z_1^{2} + z_1 z_2$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $z_2$.**
$$\dot{z}_2 = \dot{x}_2 - \dot{\alpha}_1 = (2 + \cos x_1)\,u - \dot{\alpha}_1$$
The derivative $\dot{\alpha}_1$ is computable from the known form of $\alpha_1$:
$$\dot{\alpha}_1 = -c_1 \dot{z}_1 - 2x_1\dot{x}_1 - \sin(t) = -c_1(-c_1 z_1 + z_2) - 2x_1(x_1^{2} + x_2) - \sin(t)$$

**Step 4 — Augmented Lyapunov function and design of $u$.**
$$V_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}z_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}z_2^{2}$$
$$\dot{V}_2 = -c_1 z_1^{2} + z_1 z_2 + z_2\big[(2 + \cos x_1)u - \dot{\alpha}_1\big]$$
$$= -c_1 z_1^{2} + z_2\big[z_1 + (2 + \cos x_1)u - \dot{\alpha}_1\big]$$

Choose the control to cancel the bracketed term:
$$u = \frac{1}{2 + \cos x_1}\big[-c_2 z_2 - z_1 + \dot{\alpha}_1\big], \quad c_2 > 0$$
(With $2 + \cos x_1 \in [1, 3]$, the control direction is guaranteed and $u$ is well-defined.)

**Step 5 — Substitute and conclude.**
$$\dot{V}_2 = -c_1 z_1^{2} - c_2 z_2^{2} \le 0$$
Since $V_2$ is positive definite, radially unbounded, and $\dot{V}_2$ is negative definite, the equilibrium $(z_1,z_2)=(0,0)$ is **globally asymptotically stable** by Lyapunov's direct method. Hence $x_1(t) \to \sin(t)$ and $x_2(t) \to \alpha_1(t)$ as $t \to \infty$. $\blacksquare$

#### (b) (i) State the **frozen-gain principle** and (ii) write a short note on **Gain Scheduling** with a real-time industrial example. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Frozen-Gain Principle.** For a linear-parameter-varying (LPV) system $\dot{x} = A(\rho(t))\,x + B(\rho(t))\,u$ with scheduling variable $\rho(t)$:
- If $\rho(t)$ is *slowly varying* (formally $\dot{\rho} \to 0$), the closed-loop eigenvalues drift *slowly* with $\rho$.
- If $A_{cl}(\rho_i)$ is Hurwitz for every frozen $\rho_i$ in the operating envelope *and* the system is *contracting* at each frozen point, then the time-varying closed-loop system is **stable** for slow-enough $\rho(t)$.
- The principle justifies replacing a global nonlinear stability proof by a *family of local linear analyses* — but it is only a *sufficient* condition; it fails for fast $\rho$ variations.

**(ii) Gain Scheduling with industrial example.** Gain scheduling is a *practical, widely used* nonlinear control design in which:
1. The plant is linearized at $N$ chosen operating points $\rho_i$.
2. A linear controller (PI/PID/LQR) $K_i$ is designed for each linearization.
3. During operation, the active gain is $K(\rho) = \sum \alpha_i(\rho) K_i$ with bump-shaped or linear weights $\alpha_i$.

**Real-time industrial example — F-16 aircraft autopilot (classical KTU reference):**
- Scheduling variables: Mach number $M$, altitude $h$, angle-of-attack $\alpha$.
- Operating points: typically a 3×3 grid (low/mid/high $M$ × low/mid/high $h$).
- Pre-tuned gains: roll-rate, pitch-rate, yaw-rate controllers.
- The flight computer measures $M$, $h$, $\alpha$ in real time, looks up the four nearest pre-tuned gains, and interpolates them.
- Other industrial examples: HVAC duct-pressure control, automotive active suspension, wind-turbine pitch control, robotic-manipulator joint-control with payload-dependent gains.

**Key advantage:** Gain scheduling requires *no online optimisation* — it is implementable on legacy microcontrollers. **Key limitation:** It cannot handle sudden plant faults or unmatched disturbances.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (High Marks-Loss Area)**
> - **Sliding mode:** Students often write the equivalent control but *forget the Lyapunov proof*. A 7-mark part without a Lyapunov function is capped at ~4 marks. **Always show** $V = \tfrac{1}{2}s^{2}$ and derive $\dot{V} \le -\eta \vert s \vert$.
> - **Backstepping:** A common mistake is to use $z_2 = x_2$ instead of $z_2 = x_2 - \alpha_1$. This breaks the cascade structure and yields an *incorrect* controller. The error variable *must* be $x_2 - \alpha_1$.
> - **Gain scheduling:** Do not confuse gain scheduling with *feedback linearization*. Examiners deduct marks for the two being interchanged.
> - **Chattering:** Writing "use a low-pass filter" *without specifying the cut-off frequency and trade-off* is treated as a vague answer. Specify boundary-layer width $\Phi$ and the resulting accuracy $\vert s \vert \le \Phi$.
> - **Tracking:** Always state the *exosystem* (step, ramp, sinusoid) explicitly — examiners check whether you applied the Internal Model Principle.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Gain scheduling** = *bank of linear controllers* + *gain interpolation* on a slow scheduling variable $\rho(t)$. **Frozen-gain principle** justifies stability for slow $\rho$.
- [ ] **Tracking** = $e = y - y_r \to 0$. **Internal Model Principle** = controller must contain a copy of the reference *exosystem*.
- [ ] **Sliding surface** $s = c^{T}\tilde{x}$ must be **Hurwitz** on $s=0$.
- [ ] **Equivalent control** $u_{eq} = -L_f s / L_g s$ (assumes $L_g s \neq 0$).
- [ ] **SMC law** $u = u_{eq} - K\,\text{sgn}(s)$ with $K > D + \eta$ rejects matched disturbances up to magnitude $D$.
- [ ] **Reaching condition** $s\,\dot{s} \le -\eta \vert s \vert \Rightarrow$ *finite-time reach*.
- [ ] **Chattering** is the practical Achilles' heel of SMC. Suppress via **boundary layer** or **HOSM** (Twisting, Super-Twisting).
- [ ] **Backstepping** requires **strict-feedback form** — nonlinearity in $\dot{x}_i$ must depend only on $(x_1,\dots,x_i)$.
- [ ] **Backstepping recipe:** (1) $z_1 = x_1 - x_{1d}$, (2) virtual $\alpha_1$ with $c_1>0$, (3) $z_2 = x_2 - \alpha_1$, (4) augment $V$, (5) design $u$ to make $\dot{V} \le 0$.
- [ ] **Composite Lyapunov** $V_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \tfrac{1}{2}z_i^{2}$ with $\dot{V}_k = -\sum c_i z_i^{2}$ proves *asymptotic stability* at every recursion.
- [ ] **Adaptive backstepping** = backstepping + online parameter update law when the nonlinearity is *parametrically uncertain* but linearly parameterised.
- [ ] **Gain scheduling ≠ feedback linearisation.** The former is a *lookup-based linear design*; the latter is an *exact algebraic cancellation* of nonlinearities.
- [ ] Always cite a **real-world example** in KTU 14-mark answers (F-16 autopilot, robotic manipulator, DC-DC converter) — examiners reward application awareness.
- [ ] Memory aid — "**SMC reaches fast, Backstepping constructs slow**": SMC uses a *switching* law, backstepping uses *smooth virtual controls*; both are Lyapunov-certified.
